1:
"And Joshua rose early in the morning; and they removed from Shittim, and came to Jordan, he and all the children of Israel, and lodged there before they passed over." The very brief verse notes Joshua bringing Israel to the bank of Jordan before the crossing. The grammatical voices note the approach. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joshua rose early in the morning; and they removed from Shittim, and came to Jordan... and lodged there before they passed over" — Joshua led the people from Shittim to the Jordan, where they camped before crossing; the and Joshua rose early in the morning (the early rising); the and they removed from Shittim, and came to Jordan (the march to the river); the he and all the children of Israel, and lodged there before they passed over (the camp at the bank); the figure (the diligent leader rising early, the people brought to the brink of the crossing; the truth that Joshua led the people to the edge of the promise, ready to enter; the figure of the approach to the inheritance, the river still to be crossed; the camp at the Jordan, the wonder at hand; the preparation for the crossing, vv.2). The verse holds together the approach to Jordan.

On "And Joshua rose early in the morning; and they removed from Shittim, and came to Jordan," the witnesses note the march. Gill notes Joshua leading the people to the Jordan (the approach) — the march (to Jordan).

On "he and all the children of Israel, and lodged there before they passed over," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the camp: "lodged there before they passed over" — the camp at the bank before crossing (the preparation); the figure (the people at the brink of the promise); the providence (the approach, the officers' command to follow the ark to follow, vv.2-3). The verse displays Joshua bringing Israel to the bank of Jordan.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The approach displays the people brought to the brink of the promise. The verse displays the leader: diligent. The verse displays the river: to be crossed. The verse displays the wonder: near. Joshua bringing Israel to the bank of Jordan displays the approach to the inheritance: Joshua rose early in the morning; and they removed from Shittim, and came to Jordan... and lodged there before they passed over — Joshua leading the people from Shittim to the Jordan, the camp at the brink of the promise, the wonder of the crossing at hand. The verse displays the officers' command to follow the ark when they see it move (vv.2-4).
2:
"And it came to pass after three days, that the officers went through the host;" The very brief verse notes the officers going through the host after three days. The grammatical voices note the order. The verse holds together the officers' charge.

The verse displays the officers going through the host.

3:
"And they commanded the people, saying, When ye see the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God, and the priests the Levites bearing it, then ye shall remove from your place, and go after it." The very brief verse notes the command to follow the ark when it moved. The grammatical voices note the charge. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when ye see the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God, and the priests the Levites bearing it, then ye shall remove from your place, and go after it" — the people were to follow the ark, the token of God's presence, when it moved; the and they commanded the people, saying, When ye see the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God (the ark seen); the and the priests the Levites bearing it (the ark borne by the priests); the then ye shall remove from your place, and go after it (following the ark); the figure (the people to follow the ark, the symbol of God's presence leading them; the truth that the crossing was led by the ark, God going before his people; the figure of the church following the presence of God, the ark a type of Christ leading the way, "he goeth before them," John 10:4; the people following where God leads, the ark before them; the leadership of God in the crossing, vv.4). The verse holds together following the ark.

On "And they commanded the people, saying, When ye see the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God, and the priests the Levites bearing it," the witnesses note the ark. Gill notes the ark, the token of God's presence, borne before them (the leading, John 10:4) — the ark (seen).

The verse displays the command to follow the ark when it moved.

4:
"Yet there shall be a space between you and it, about two thousand cubits by measure: come not near unto it, that ye may know the way by which ye must go: for ye have not passed this way heretofore." The very brief verse notes the space kept from the ark, that they might know the way. The grammatical voices note the distance. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "there shall be a space between you and it, about two thousand cubits by measure: come not near unto it, that ye may know the way by which ye must go" — a distance was to be kept from the ark, that all might see it lead the unknown way; the yet there shall be a space between you and it, about two thousand cubits by measure (the distance); the come not near unto it (the reverence); the that ye may know the way by which ye must go: for ye have not passed this way heretofore (the ark showing the unknown way); the figure (the distance kept that all might see the ark lead, the reverence due to God's presence; the truth that the ark went before to show the way they had not known; the figure of the guidance of God in the unknown path, the ark before them, "I will lead them in paths that they have not known," Isaiah 42:16; the reverence and the guidance together, the way made known; the new and untried way led by God, vv.3). The verse holds together the ark showing the way.

5:
"And Joshua said unto the people, Sanctify yourselves: for to morrow the LORD will do wonders among you." The witnesses note Joshua's call to sanctify themselves, for the LORD would do wonders — the crux of the preparation of holiness for the great works of God. On "sanctify yourselves: for to morrow the LORD will do wonders among you," interpreters expound the call. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the preparation of holiness — "and Joshua said unto the people, Sanctify yourselves: for to morrow the LORD will do wonders among you": the people were to consecrate themselves in readiness for the wonder God would work; the witnesses note the significance — the call to holiness and consecration as the fitting preparation for the works of God (the people to sanctify themselves, by washing and putting away defilement, that they be ready to behold and share in the wonder); the lesson — that those who would see and receive the works of God must prepare themselves in holiness, separating from sin and consecrating themselves to him (the wonder of God met by a sanctified people, the inward and outward preparation); the truth that holiness is the fitting frame for the presence and works of God, "follow... holiness, without which no man shall see the Lord," Hebrews 12:14; the figure — the consecration of God's people in expectation of his great deeds, "cleanse yourselves... be ye clean, that bear the vessels of the LORD," Isaiah 52:11; the truth that God's wonders are wrought among a people prepared, the heart cleansed for his working; the figure of the readiness of faith that sanctifies itself for what God will do; the call to put away sin and draw near in holiness before the great work; the warning that the unsanctified are unfit to behold the works of God. The figure of the preparation of holiness for the great works of God.

On "and Joshua said unto the people, Sanctify yourselves," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the consecration. Keil and Ellicott note the people called to sanctify and consecrate themselves, putting away defilement, in readiness for God's work (the preparation of holiness, Isaiah 52:11) — the call (sanctify yourselves).

The verse displays the priests commanded to take up the ark and go before the people (v.6).
6:

The verse holds together the ark borne before the people.

7:

The verse holds together God magnifying Joshua.

8:

10:
"And Joshua said, Hereby ye shall know that the living God is among you, and that he will without fail drive out from before you the Canaanites, and the Hittites, and the Hivites, and the Perizzites, and the Girgashites, and the Amorites, and the Jebusites." 11:
"Behold, the ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth passeth over before you into Jordan." The witnesses note that the ark of the Lord of all the earth would pass over before them — the crux of the presence of the universal Sovereign leading his people. On "the ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth passeth over before you into Jordan," interpreters expound the title. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the Lord of all the earth — "behold, the ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth passeth over before you into Jordan": the ark, the token of the presence of him who is Lord of the whole earth, would go before them into the river; the witnesses note the significance — the presence and sovereignty of God, the Lord of all the earth, leading his people; the lesson — that the God who leads his people is Lord of all the earth, sovereign over nature and the nations, able to part the waters and drive out the Canaanites; the truth that the whole earth is the LORD's, "the earth is the LORD's, and the fulness thereof," Psalm 24:1; "the LORD of the whole earth," Zechariah 4:14; the figure — the Sovereign of all the earth going before his people, the presence that commands creation. The figure of the presence of the universal Sovereign, the Lord of all the earth, leading his people.

On "Behold, the ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the title. Keil and Ellicott note the ark the token of the presence of him who is Lord of the whole earth, not a local God but the Sovereign of all (the universal dominion, Psalm 24:1) — the title (the Lord of all the earth).

On "passeth over before you into Jordan," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the leading. Calvin and Gill note the ark going before them into the river, the Sovereign of the whole world commanding the Jordan and leading his people (the presence that commands creation, Zechariah 4:14) — the figure (Christ the Lord of all leading his people through the flood); the providence (the title, the twelve men chosen and the waters to be cut off to follow, vv.12-13). The verse displays that the ark of the Lord of all the earth would pass over before them.

The witnesses agree the substance: the ark, the token of the presence of the Lord of all the earth, would go before them into the river (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the universal Sovereign's presence: the Lord of all the earth — the presence and sovereignty of God leading his people, the ark not of a local deity but of the Sovereign of the whole world, who commands the river and the nations, the whole earth the LORD's (Psalm 24:1; Zechariah 4:14), a figure of Christ the Lord of all leading his people through every flood, the title that grounds the certainty of the parting of the waters and the conquest. The verse displays the universal-Sovereign crux. That the ark of the Lord of all the earth would pass over before them displays the crux: the ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth passeth over before you into Jordan (Psalm 24:1; Zechariah 4:14) — the presence and sovereignty of the Lord of the whole world leading his people into the river, the Sovereign who commands creation and the nations, a figure of Christ the Lord of all. The verse displays the twelve men chosen, one of each tribe, for the memorial (vv.12).
12:
"Now therefore take you twelve men out of the tribes of Israel, out of every tribe a man." The very brief verse notes the twelve men chosen, one of each tribe. The grammatical voices note the choosing. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "take you twelve men out of the tribes of Israel, out of every tribe a man" — twelve men, one from each tribe, were chosen for the memorial of the crossing; the now therefore take you twelve men out of the tribes of Israel (the twelve men); the out of every tribe a man (one of each tribe); the figure (the twelve men, representing all the tribes, chosen for the memorial; the truth that all Israel was to have a part in the memorial, one from each tribe; the figure of the whole people represented in the twelve, the memorial of all; the men chosen to take up the stones, vv.4; the twelve a token of the united nation, all sharing in the wonder). The verse holds together the twelve men chosen.

On "Now therefore take you twelve men out of the tribes of Israel," the witnesses note the choosing. Gill notes the twelve men chosen for the memorial (the representatives) — the choosing (twelve men).

On "out of every tribe a man," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the tribes: "out of every tribe a man" — one of each tribe, all represented (the united nation); the figure (the whole people in the twelve); the providence (the choosing, the waters to be cut off to follow, vv.13). The verse displays the twelve men chosen, one of each tribe.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the choosing — "take you twelve men out of the tribes of Israel, out of every tribe a man," the choosing (Keil, Gill); "Now therefore take you twelve men out of the tribes of Israel," the twelve (Gill); "out of every tribe a man," the tribes (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the choosing. The verse marks the twelve: chosen. The verse displays the tribes: each represented. The verse displays the memorial: prepared. The verse displays the nation: united. The choosing displays all Israel represented in the twelve. The verse displays the men: selected. The verse displays the tribes: all. The verse displays the part: shared. The twelve men chosen, one of each tribe, display the whole people represented in the memorial: take you twelve men out of the tribes of Israel, out of every tribe a man — twelve men, one from each tribe, chosen for the memorial of the crossing, all Israel having a part, the twelve a token of the united nation. The verse displays that the waters of Jordan would be cut off when the priests' feet rested in them (v.13).
13:
"And it shall come to pass, as soon as the soles of the feet of the priests that bear the ark of the LORD, the Lord of all the earth, shall rest in the waters of Jordan, that the waters of Jordan shall be cut off from the waters that come down from above; and they shall stand upon an heap." The very brief verse notes the promise that the waters would be cut off when the priests' feet touched them. The grammatical voices note the promise. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "as soon as the soles of the feet of the priests that bear the ark... shall rest in the waters of Jordan, that the waters of Jordan shall be cut off... and they shall stand upon an heap" — when the priests' feet touched the water, the river would be cut off and stand in a heap; the and it shall come to pass, as soon as the soles of the feet of the priests... shall rest in the waters of Jordan (the priests' feet touching the water); the that the waters of Jordan shall be cut off from the waters that come down from above (the waters cut off); the and they shall stand upon an heap (the waters standing in a heap); the figure (the wonder that the waters would part the moment the priests' feet touched them, the faith that precedes the miracle; "by faith they passed through the Red sea," Hebrews 11:29; the miracle that confirmed God's presence, vv.16). The verse holds together the waters cut off.

On "And it shall come to pass, as soon as the soles of the feet of the priests that bear the ark of the LORD, the Lord of all the earth, shall rest in the waters of Jordan," the witnesses note the touch. Gill notes the priests' feet touching the water (the step of faith, Hebrews 11:29) — the touch (the feet in the water).

On "that the waters of Jordan shall be cut off from the waters that come down from above; and they shall stand upon an heap," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the parting: "the waters of Jordan shall be cut off... and they shall stand upon an heap" — the waters cut off and standing in a heap (the wonder following faith); the figure (the faith that goes forward and finds the way opened); the providence (the promise, the people passing over on dry ground to follow, vv.14-17).

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "as soon as the soles of the feet of the priests... shall rest in the waters of Jordan, that the waters of Jordan shall be cut off," the promise (Keil, Gill); "And it shall come to pass, as soon as the soles of the feet of the priests that bear the ark of the LORD, the Lord of all the earth, shall rest in the waters of Jordan," the touch (Hebrews 11:29, Gill); "that the waters of Jordan shall be cut off from the waters that come down from above; and they shall stand upon an heap," the parting (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The promise displays the waters parting at the step of faith. The promise that the waters would be cut off when the priests' feet touched them displays the faith that precedes the miracle: as soon as the soles of the feet of the priests... shall rest in the waters of Jordan, that the waters of Jordan shall be cut off (Hebrews 11:29) — the river to be cut off and stand in a heap the moment the priests' feet touched the water, the wonder following the step of faith, the Lord of all the earth commanding the river.14-15).
14:
"And it came to pass, when the people removed from their tents, to pass over Jordan, and the priests bearing the ark of the covenant before the people;" The very brief verse notes the people setting out with the priests bearing the ark before them. The grammatical voices note the setting out. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when the people removed from their tents, to pass over Jordan, and the priests bearing the ark of the covenant before the people" — the people set out to cross, the priests bearing the ark before them; the and it came to pass, when the people removed from their tents, to pass over Jordan (the people setting out); the and the priests bearing the ark of the covenant before the people (the ark leading); the figure (the people advancing to cross, the ark borne before them; the truth that the crossing began, the ark leading the way; the figure of the people going forward at God's command, the presence before them; the obedience that sets out at the word, the ark first; the advance to the river, vv.6). The verse holds together the people setting out.

On "And it came to pass, when the people removed from their tents, to pass over Jordan," the witnesses note the setting out. Gill notes the people setting out to cross (the advance) — the setting out (from their tents).

On "and the priests bearing the ark of the covenant before the people," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the ark: "the priests bearing the ark of the covenant before the people" — the ark borne before the people (God leading); the figure (the presence before them); the providence (the advance, the priests' feet dipping in the brim of the water to follow, vv.15). The verse displays the people setting out with the priests bearing the ark.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the setting out — "when the people removed from their tents, to pass over Jordan, and the priests bearing the ark of the covenant before the people," the setting out (Keil, Gill); "And it came to pass, when the people removed from their tents, to pass over Jordan," the advance (Gill); "and the priests bearing the ark of the covenant before the people," the ark (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the setting out. The verse marks the people: removing. The verse displays the crossing: begun. The verse displays the ark: leading. The verse displays the priests: bearing it. The setting out displays the people advancing at God's command. The verse displays the advance: to the river. The verse displays the ark: before them. The verse displays the obedience: shown. The people setting out with the priests bearing the ark display the advance at God's command: when the people removed from their tents, to pass over Jordan, and the priests bearing the ark of the covenant before the people — the people setting out to cross, the ark borne before them, the obedience that goes forward at the word, the presence of God leading the way. The verse displays the priests' feet dipping in the brim of the water at harvest, when Jordan overflowed (v.15).
15:
"And as they that bare the ark were come unto Jordan, and the feet of the priests that bare the ark were dipped in the brim of the water, (for Jordan overfloweth all his banks all the time of harvest,)" The very brief verse notes the priests' feet dipping in the brim of the overflowing Jordan. The grammatical voices note the moment. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "as they that bare the ark were come unto Jordan, and the feet of the priests... were dipped in the brim of the water, (for Jordan overfloweth all his banks all the time of harvest)" — the priests stepped into the water just as the river was in full flood at harvest; the and as they that bare the ark were come unto Jordan, and the feet of the priests that bare the ark were dipped in the brim of the water (the feet in the water); the (for Jordan overfloweth all his banks all the time of harvest) (the river in full flood); the figure (the priests stepping into the flooded river, the wonder the greater for the flood; the truth that the river was at its highest, the miracle the more glorious; the figure of the faith that steps into the flood, the wonder met at the brim, "the waters saw thee... they were afraid," Psalm 77:16; the river in flood, the step of faith taken; the miracle at the moment of the touch, vv.13). The verse holds together the feet in the flood.

On "And as they that bare the ark were come unto Jordan, and the feet of the priests that bare the ark were dipped in the brim of the water," the witnesses note the step. Gill notes the priests' feet dipped in the water (the step, Psalm 77:16) — the step (feet in the brim).

On "(for Jordan overfloweth all his banks all the time of harvest,)" the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the flood: "for Jordan overfloweth all his banks all the time of harvest" — the river in full flood at harvest (the wonder the greater); the figure (the faith that steps into the flood); the providence (the moment, the waters standing in a heap to follow, vv.16). The verse displays the priests' feet dipping in the brim of the overflowing Jordan.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the moment — "the feet of the priests... were dipped in the brim of the water, (for Jordan overfloweth all his banks)," the moment (Keil, Gill); "And as they that bare the ark were come unto Jordan, and the feet of the priests that bare the ark were dipped in the brim of the water," the step (Psalm 77:16, Gill); "(for Jordan overfloweth all his banks all the time of harvest,)" the flood (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the moment. The verse marks the feet: dipped. The verse displays the river: in flood. The verse displays the wonder: the greater. The verse displays the faith: stepping in. The moment displays the step of faith into the flooded river. The verse displays the feet: in the brim. The verse displays the flood: full. The verse displays the miracle: at hand. The priests' feet dipping in the brim of the overflowing Jordan display the step of faith into the flood: the feet of the priests that bare the ark were dipped in the brim of the water, (for Jordan overfloweth all his banks all the time of harvest) (Psalm 77:16) — the priests stepping into the water at the river's full flood, the wonder the greater for the flood, the step of faith taken at the brim. The verse displays the waters standing and rising up in a heap (vv.16).
16:
"That the waters which came down from above stood and rose up upon an heap very far from the city Adam, that is beside Zaretan: and those that came down toward the sea of the plain, even the salt sea, failed, and were cut off: and the people passed over right against Jericho." The very brief verse notes the waters standing in a heap so the people passed over against Jericho. The grammatical voices note the miracle. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the waters which came down from above stood and rose up upon an heap very far from the city Adam... and those that came down toward the sea of the plain... failed, and were cut off: and the people passed over right against Jericho" — the upper waters stood in a heap and the lower drained away, so the people crossed on dry ground; the that the waters which came down from above stood and rose up upon an heap very far from the city Adam, that is beside Zaretan (the upper waters heaped up); the and those that came down toward the sea of the plain, even the salt sea, failed, and were cut off (the lower waters drained); the and the people passed over right against Jericho (the crossing); the figure (the great miracle, the river divided, the people crossing over against the doomed city; the truth that God divided the Jordan as he had the Red Sea, the waters heaped up; the figure of the way opened through the flood, the people passing safely, "he divided the sea... and made the waters to stand as an heap," Psalm 78:13; the river parted, the people over against Jericho; the wonder that opened the way into the land, vv.13). The verse holds together the waters heaped up.

On "That the waters which came down from above stood and rose up upon an heap very far from the city Adam, that is beside Zaretan," the witnesses note the heap. Gill notes the upper waters standing in a heap (the miracle, Psalm 78:13) — the heap (the waters rose up).

On "and those that came down toward the sea of the plain, even the salt sea, failed, and were cut off: and the people passed over right against Jericho," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the crossing: "those that came down... failed, and were cut off: and the people passed over right against Jericho" — the lower waters drained and the people crossed (the way opened); the figure (the way through the flood, the people safe); the providence (the miracle, the people passing over on dry ground to follow, vv.17).

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the miracle — "the waters which came down from above stood and rose up upon an heap... and the people passed over right against Jericho," the miracle (Keil, Gill); "That the waters which came down from above stood and rose up upon an heap very far from the city Adam, that is beside Zaretan," the heap (Psalm 78:13, Gill); "and those that came down toward the sea of the plain, even the salt sea, failed, and were cut off: and the people passed over right against Jericho," the crossing (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The miracle displays the river divided as the Red Sea. The waters standing in a heap so the people passed over against Jericho display the way opened through the flood: the waters which came down from above stood and rose up upon an heap... and the people passed over right against Jericho (Psalm 78:13) — the upper waters heaped up and the lower drained, the river divided as the Red Sea, the people crossing on the opened way over against the doomed city. The verse displays the priests standing firm on dry ground while all Israel passed over (v.17).
17:
"And the priests that bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD stood firm on dry ground in the midst of Jordan, and all the Israelites passed over on dry ground, until all the people were passed clean over Jordan." The witnesses note that the priests stood firm and all Israel passed over on dry ground — the crux of the great deliverance through the waters, fulfilled in the Red Sea and a figure of salvation. On "the priests... stood firm on dry ground in the midst of Jordan, and all the Israelites passed over on dry ground," interpreters expound the wonder. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the dry-ground passage — "and the priests that bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD stood firm on dry ground in the midst of Jordan, and all the Israelites passed over on dry ground, until all the people were passed clean over Jordan": the priests stood firm with the ark in the dry riverbed while the whole nation crossed over on dry ground; the witnesses note the significance — the complete and glorious deliverance of all Israel through the parted waters, the priests bearing the ark standing firm while the people passed; the lesson — that God opens a way for his people through every barrier, the waters that should have drowned them made dry land beneath their feet; "thou leddest thy people... through the deep, as an horse in the wilderness," Isaiah 63:13; Psalm 66:6; the figure — the passage through the waters a figure of salvation, of baptism, and of the believer's passage through death into the inheritance, "they were all baptized... in the sea," 1 Corinthians 10:1-2. The figure of the great deliverance through the waters, all Israel passing over on dry ground.

On "and the priests that bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD stood firm on dry ground in the midst of Jordan," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the priests standing firm. Keil and Ellicott note the priests with the ark standing firm in the dry riverbed, the presence of God holding back the flood — the standing (firm on dry ground).

On "and all the Israelites passed over on dry ground, until all the people were passed clean over Jordan," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the deliverance. Calvin and Gill note the whole nation passing over on dry ground, the deliverance complete and none lost, answering to the Red Sea, a figure of salvation and baptism — the figure (Christ the Ark standing firm while his people pass through); the providence (the crossing, the twelve stones taken up for the memorial to follow, ch.4). The verse displays that the priests stood firm and all Israel passed over on dry ground.

The witnesses agree the substance: the priests stood firm with the ark while the whole nation passed over on dry ground (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the great deliverance: passed over on dry ground — the complete and glorious deliverance of all Israel through the parted waters (Psalm 66:6), answering to the passage of the Red Sea (Isaiah 63:13), a figure of salvation and baptism (1 Corinthians 10:1-2), Christ the Ark standing firm in the midst while his people pass through into the inheritance. The verse displays the dry-ground crux. That the priests stood firm and all Israel passed over on dry ground displays the crux: the priests... stood firm on dry ground in the midst of Jordan, and all the Israelites passed over on dry ground (Psalm 66:6; Isaiah 63:13; 1 Corinthians 10:1-2), a figure of salvation and of the believer's passage into the inheritance. The verse displays the command to take twelve stones from the midst of Jordan for a memorial (ch.4).
